provenance.source_corpus: Natural Recovery of Rough Fescue ( Festuca hallii (Vasey) Piper) Grassland After Disturbance by Pipeline Construction in Central Alberta, Canada

source_spans.text: Pipeline construction strips vegetation, leaving subsoil on the surface, admixing soil horizons, compacting soils, and destroying biological crusts including moss and lichen (Naeth et al. 1987; Lovich and Bainbridge 1999).

Pipeline construction strips vegetation, leaving subsoil on the surface, admixing soil horizons, compacting soils, and destroying biological crusts including moss and lichen (Naeth et al. 1987; Lovich and Bainbridge 1999).

nodes.name: biological crusts including moss and lichen

source_spans.text: Pipeline construction strips vegetation, leaving subsoil on the surface, admixing soil horizons, compacting soils, and destroying biological crusts including moss and lichen (Naeth et al. 1987; Lovich and Bainbridge 1999).

Pipeline construction techniques affected F. hallii cover, which was significantly greater on plow-in pipelines, and wheatgrass cover, which was significantly greater on ditch-witch and topsoil-strip pipelines (Figure 3).

Pipeline construction techniques affected F. hallii cover, which was significantly greater on plow-in pipelines, and wheatgrass cover, which was significantly greater on ditch-witch and topsoil-strip pipelines (Figure 3).

Pipeline construction techniques affected F. hallii cover, which was significantly greater on plow-in pipelines, and wheatgrass cover, which was significantly greater on ditch-witch and topsoil-strip pipelines (Figure 3).
Festuca hallii, moss and lichen, and litter cover were significantly greater on controls than ROWs (Figure 4).
Festuca hallii, moss and lichen, and litter cover were significantly greater on controls than ROWs (Figure 4).
Festuca hallii, moss and lichen, and litter cover were significantly greater on controls than ROWs (Figure 4).
Festuca hallii, moss and lichen, and litter cover were significantly greater on controls than ROWs (Figure 4).
Wheatgrasses ( Elymus lanceolatus, E. trachycaulus, and P . smithii) and bare ground cover were significantly lower on controls than ROWs (Figure 4).
Plow-in pipelines had the fewest non-native species, greatest rough fescue recovery, and were similar to native grassland.
Seeding native rough fescue (Festuca hallii (Vasey) Piper or Festuca campestris Rydb.) has had no documented success.
Seeding native rough fescue (Festuca hallii (Vasey) Piper or Festuca campestris Rydb.) has had no documented success.
